summary: An indigenous people of central and southern Chile and western Argentina, centered on the Araucania region south of the Bio-Bio River and speaking the language isolate Mapudungun. Organized around chiefs called lonco who elected temporary wartime leaders called toqui (or toki), the Mapuche fought the Spanish in the Arauco War, killing the conquistador Pedro de Valdivia and carrying out the Destruction of the Seven Cities, in what is sometimes called the world's longest war against a colonial power (1536-1883). They lost independence only in the 19th century, to Chile's Occupation of Araucania and Argentina's Conquest of the Desert under Julio Argentino Roca, and remain politically active today around land rights and autonomy.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the Mapuche are identified in giveaways: an indigenous South American people centered in central and southern Chile, with a presence in western/southern Argentina, whose homeland Araucania lies south of the Bio-Bio River and extends to the Chiloe Archipelago. They speak Mapudungun, a language isolate, and are most often identified by their killing of the conquistador Pedro de Valdivia and their long, largely successful resistance to Spanish and later Chilean/Argentine conquest, sometimes called the world's longest war against a colonial power.

clues.clue: An indigenous people of central/southern Chile and western/southern Argentina
examples: For 10 points, name these indigenous South American people of Chile and western Argentina.
For 10 points, name this indigenous ethnic group that has frequently rebelled against the government of Chile.

clues.clue: Homeland is the Araucania region south of the Bio-Bio River; also lived in the Chiloe Archipelago
examples: These people live mostly south of the Bio-Bio River in the Araucania region, and they have also lived in the Chiloe Archipelago.
This people's territory was bordered to the north by the Bio-Bio River.

clues.clue: Speak Mapudungun, a language isolate
examples: These people lived around the town of Temuco and the River Bio-Bio, and they speak a language isolate.
These speakers of the Mapudungun language refer to descendants of Spanish colonists as wingka, a term derived from "new Incas".

clues.clue: Killed the conquistador Pedro de Valdivia during the Spanish conquest of Chile
examples: Pedro de Valdivia conquered what native people of Southern Chile?
These people were led by chieftains called lonco, who appointed military leaders called toqui, who defeated and killed the conquistador Pedro de Valdivia.

clues.clue: Fought the Spanish (and later Chile/Argentina) in what is sometimes called the world's longest war, lasting from 1536 to 1883
examples: This group from southern Chile fought the world's longest war against the Spanish, lasting from 1536 to 1883.

works.name: Society and Political Structure
works.description: Mapuche political authority was decentralized and organized around local chiefs called lonco (also holding authority alongside figures called ulmen), who in wartime would meet to elect a single temporary military leader, the toqui (or toki). Prominent chiefs employed silversmiths called ruxafe, and the Mapuche negotiated repeated parliaments with the Spanish, at which outsiders were made to bury their weapons and plant a canelo tree, including the Treaty of Quillin.

clues.clue: Chiefs called lonco would meet to elect a single, temporary wartime leader, the toqui (or toki)
examples: Chiefs among these people called loncos would meet to elect a single wartime leader, or toqui, during events such as the "Destruction of the Seven Cities."
These people elected military leaders called tokis in times of crisis.

clues.clue: Positions of authority also included the ulmen, alongside the lonko
examples: Positions of authority within this people's society included the ulmen and lonko.

clues.clue: Prominent chiefs hired silversmiths called ruxafe
examples: Many of these people's prominent chiefs hired silversmiths called ruxafe.

clues.clue: Held parliaments with the Spanish at which outsiders were forced to bury their weapons and plant a canelo tree on top
examples: This people conducted a number of parliaments with a society that surrounded them in which this people would force the outsiders to bury their weapons and plant a canelo tree on top.

clues.clue: Signed the Treaty of Quillin with a European power
examples: Wartime leaders of this people were called Toqui and this group later signed the Treaty of Quillin with a European power.

clues.clue: The northern branch's lack of advanced agriculture led northern neighbors to nickname them "promauca," meaning wild wolves
examples: The lack of advanced agriculture among the northern branch of this people lead their northern neighbors to nickname them the promauca meaning wild wolves.

clues.clue: Refer to descendants of Spanish colonists as wingka, a term derived from "new Incas"
examples: These speakers of the Mapudungun language refer to descendants of Spanish colonists as wingka, a term derived from "new Incas".

works.name: The Arauco War: Lautaro, Caupolican, and Pedro de Valdivia
works.description: The Arauco War opened with Mapuche resistance to Pedro de Valdivia's conquest, defeating a Spanish force at the Battle of Tucapel — where they used giant clava clubs and organized spearman formations against cavalry — and killing Valdivia himself. After the toqui Lautaro's death, Caupolican led an uprising against colonial forces; the war produced individual acts of Mapuche resistance still cited in questions today, and was sympathetically fictionalized by the Spanish poet Alonso de Ercilla in the epic La Araucana, now Chile's national epic.

clues.clue: At the Battle of Tucapel, they killed Pedro de Valdivia, led by their toqui, Lautaro
examples: One expedition against these people was defeated at the Battle of Tucapel by their toqui, or war chief, Lautaro.
Name these people, who were led by the toqui Lautaro to a victory in the Battle of Tucapel.

clues.clue: Used giant clavas (traditional clubs) and organized spearman formations to defeat Spanish cavalry at the Battle of Tucapel
examples: These people used giant clavas, a type of traditional club, and organized formations of spearmen to defeat cavalry at the Battle of Tucapel under their leader Caupolican.

clues.clue: Pedro de Valdivia mutilated captured warriors by cutting off their toes, at El Valle de la Mocha, before his own death
examples: These people launched malon cattle raids and had their toes cut off by a commander who was killed after the Battle of Tucapel by Caupolican.
Pedro de Valdivia supposedly cut the toes off of an invading force of these people at El Valle de la Mocha, where they were led by Lautaro.

clues.clue: Caupolican led a rebellion against colonial forces after the death of Lautaro
examples: This group was lead by Caupolican after the death of Lautaro during a rebellion against colonial forces.

clues.clue: A warrior named Colo Colo now names a popular soccer team
examples: A third warrior of these people, Colo Colo, names a popular soccer team.

clues.clue: A warrior kicked an executioner off a platform so he could jump onto a spike himself rather than be pushed
examples: A warrior of these people kicked an executioner off a platform so that he could jump onto a giant spike, rather than be pushed onto it.

clues.clue: A captured warrior, after having both hands amputated, tied knives (or swords) to his stumps to keep fighting
examples: After both of his hands were amputated, a warrior among these people tied two knives to his wrists to keep on fighting.
After being captured in battle, a warrior of this people had both of his hands chopped off, but upon being released, continued to fight with swords tied to his stumps.

clues.clue: Sympathetically fictionalized by Alonso de Ercilla in the epic La Araucana, now Chile's national epic, which fictionalized their leader Lautaro
examples: Attempted conquerors of this people included Alonso de Ercilla, whose sympathetic account of them is now their native country's national epic.
An epic by Alonso de Ercilla fictionalized a leader of these people named Lautaro.

clues.clue: Catalina de Erauso, the "Lieutenant Nun," received the rank of ensign while fighting them, passing as a man
examples: Catalina de Erauso received the rank of ensign while fighting these people, passing as a man, and later becoming immortalized as the "Lieutenant Nun."

works.name: The Destruction of the Seven Cities
works.description: In 1598, the leader Pelantaro led an uprising that culminated in the Battle of Curalaba, after which the Mapuche destroyed a group of Spanish settlements south of the Bio-Bio River known as the Seven Cities, temporarily forcing the Spanish out and fighting alongside the losing Royalists in the later Guerra a muerte ("War to the Death"). Before Spanish contact, the Mapuche had already halted the Inca Empire's southward expansion at the Maule River, having adopted agricultural techniques from Tiwanaku, though members in the north worked the Marga-Marga mine to produce tribute for the Inca.

clues.clue: Destroyed a group of Spanish settlements south of the Bio-Bio River known as the Seven Cities
examples: All Spanish settlements south of the Bio-Bio River were attacked during these people's Destruction of the Seven Cities.
Unrest among these people caused the "destruction of the seven cities" south of the Biobio River.

clues.clue: The uprising, led by Pelantaro, culminated in the 1598 Battle of Curalaba
examples: Pelantaro led an uprising of these people that ended in the Battle of Curalaba.
These people destroyed a group of settlements called the "seven cities" after winning the 1598 Battle of Curalaba.

clues.clue: Fought alongside the losing Royalists in the later Guerra a muerte ("War to the Death")
examples: This people carried out the Destruction of the Seven Cities, eliminating foreign settlements south of the Bio-Bio river, and fought with the losing royalists in the Guerra a muerte.

clues.clue: Before Spanish contact, halted the Inca Empire's expansion at the Maule River, having adopted agricultural techniques from Tiwanaku (per historian Tom Dillehay)
examples: Tom Dillehay argued that this other group adopted many of Tiwanaku's agricultural techniques. This group halted the Incas at the Maule River and with the Huilliche carried out the Destruction of the Seven Cities.

clues.clue: Members worked in the Marga-Marga mine to produce tribute for the nearby Inca Empire
examples: Members of this people worked in the Marga-Marga mine to produce tribute for a nearby empire.

works.name: Warfare and Culture
works.description: Mapuche warfare centered on the malon, a mounted cattle raid conducted after adopting the horse; the historian Guillaume Boccara classified their military actions into vindictive attacks (tautulun), defensive wars (weichan), and rapid supply raids, and the leader Lientur favored feigned retreats as a tactic. Distinctive cultural practices included the martial art Kollelaullin and, before colonial contact, plowing with the now-extinct chilihueque camelid.

clues.clue: Conducted malon cattle raids, after adopting the horse, across the Bio-Bio River frontier
examples: These people launched malon cattle raids and had their toes cut off by a commander who was killed after the Battle of Tucapel by Caupolican.
Name these indigenous people who maintained their independence through long wars with the Spanish and adopted the horse to carry out Malon raids.

clues.clue: Guillaume Boccara classed their military actions into vindictive attacks (tautulun), defensive wars (weichan), and a type of rapid supply raid
examples: Guillaume Boccara classed these people's military actions into vindictive attacks called tautulun, defensive wars called weichan, and a type of rapid supply raid.

clues.clue: The leader Lientur favored feigned retreats as a tactic during malon raids
examples: Feigned retreats were a favored tactic of Lientur, a leader of these people who led many malon cattle raids.

clues.clue: Practice a martial art called Kollelaullin, named for its performers' ant-like shape or ant-like strength-building
examples: These people practice a martial art whose name either refers to the ant-like shape of its performers or its ant-like strength-building nature, Kollelaullin.

clues.clue: Before colonial contact, used the now-extinct chilihueque camelid to plow land on Mocha Island
examples: Before colonial contact, these people used the now-extinct chilihueques to plow land on Mocha Island.

works.name: Loss of Independence: Orelie-Antoine, Araucania, and the Conquest of the Desert
works.description: The Mapuche retained independence south of the Bio-Bio River through the entire colonial era, but lost it in the 19th century. A French adventurer, Orelie-Antoine I, declared himself King of Araucania, proposing a parliament modeled on their traditional coyag assembly and promising French military aid. Chile ended their independence with the Occupation of Araucania, completed by Domingo Santa Maria's "final pacification" after the War of the Pacific, while across the Andes, Argentina's Conquest of the Desert under Julio Argentino Roca forcibly displaced them and the neighboring Tehuelche (who had been linguistically "Araucanized"), building the defensive trench known as Alsina's Ditch (Zanja de Alsina) against their raids.

clues.clue: The Frenchman Orelie-Antoine I declared himself King of Araucania over them, proposing a parliament based on their traditional coyag assembly and promising French military aid
examples: These people's traditional assembly, the coyag, was the basis for a parliament proposed by the Frenchman Orelie-Antoine I, who declared himself king of these people.
Frenchman Orelie Antoine de Tounens managed to become king of this people.

clues.clue: Chile ended their independence with the Occupation of Araucania in the mid-1800s
examples: Valdivia is located in this country, which ended Mapuche independence with the Occupation of Araucania in the mid-1800s.

clues.clue: After the War of the Pacific, Domingo Santa Maria launched the "final pacification" of them and their territory
examples: After the War of the Pacific, Domingo Santa Maria launched the final pacification of this people and their territory.

clues.clue: Julio Argentino Roca's Conquest of the Desert forcibly displaced them from their traditional Argentine territories
examples: Julio Argentino Roca's Conquest of the Desert displaced these people, who were led by toquis such as Caupolican and Lautaro when they killed the conquistador Pedro de Valdivia.
Hundreds of years of eastward migration and cultural influence put this people in the path of the Conquest of the Desert.

clues.clue: A fortified trench, Alsina's Ditch (Zanja de Alsina), was built to protect Argentine settlements from their raids
examples: A large fortified trench named after Adolfo Alsina protected against these people's malon cattle raids.
The Zanja de Alsina was a trench built to protect against the malones, or raids, this people conducted against their neighbors.

clues.clue: The neighboring Tehuelche adopted their language in a process called "Araucanization," and were also displaced by the Conquest of the Desert
examples: Neighboring people like the Tehuelche adopted the language of these people in a process termed "Araucanization".
The Tehuelche were "Araucanized" by these people, who were displaced during the Conquest of the Desert.

clues.clue: Mass northward migrations by the group were kept in check by the founding of the colonial city of La Serena
examples: Mass migrations of these people northward were kept in control by the founding of the colonial city of La Serena.

works.name: Modern Mapuche: Politics and Land Rights
works.description: The Mapuche remain politically active in contemporary Chile and Argentina. Their communal lands around Temuco were privatized under Augusto Pinochet's dictatorship, and they have since protested the use of Pinochet-era anti-terrorism laws against activists, while the militant group CAM, led by Hector Llaitul, seeks autonomy. Mapuche activist Elisa Loncon chaired Chile's 2021 constitutional convention, which reserved seats for the group but saw its proposed constitution rejected in a 2022 referendum, and Mapuche ideas have since inspired a 2024 legal-rights declaration for the Biobio river, even as conflicts continue over sites like the Ralco Hydroelectric Plant and land disputes in Argentina.

clues.clue: Communal lands around Temuco and the Bio-Bio River were privatized under Augusto Pinochet's right-wing dictatorship
examples: Name this group whose communal lands around the town of Temuco and the Bio-Bio river were privatized under a right-wing dictatorship.

clues.clue: Have protested the use of Pinochet-era anti-terrorism laws against them
examples: These people have protested the use of Pinochet-era anti-terrorism laws against them.

clues.clue: The militant group CAM, led by Hector Llaitul, seeks autonomy for the group
examples: Hector Llaitul leads the militant group CAM, which seeks autonomy for these people.

clues.clue: Member Elisa Loncon chaired a 2021 constitutional convention with seats reserved for the group; the resulting left-leaning constitution was rejected in a September 2022 referendum
examples: This ethnic group had seven reserved seats at a convention chaired by its member Elisa Loncon, whose left-leaning proposed constitution was rejected by a September 2022 referendum.

clues.clue: Their ideas inspired a 2024 declaration of legal rights for the Biobio river
examples: These people's ideas inspired a 2024 declaration of legal rights for the Biobio river.

clues.clue: In October 2024, Javier Milei's government canceled a planned ceding of national park land to the group
examples: In October 2024, a ceding of national park land to this group was canceled by Javier Milei's government.

clues.clue: Served by the radio station Petu Mongelein ("we're still alive"); activist Betiana Colhuan was expelled from her home near a sacred rewe space; conduct trawun assemblies
examples: This ethnic group is served by the radio station Petu Mongelein, whose name translates to "we're still alive." Betiana Colhuan became an activist for these people after being expelled from her home near a sacred rewe space.

clues.clue: The flooding of one of their graveyards by the Ralco Hydroelectric Plant on the Bio-Bio River sparked an ongoing conflict
examples: An ongoing conflict began when a graveyard of these people was flooded by the Ralco Hydroelectric Plant on the Bio-Bio River.

comprehensive_summary: The Mapuche are an indigenous people of central and southern Chile and western/southern Argentina, centered on the Araucania region south of the Bio-Bio River and extending to the Chiloe Archipelago, and speaking Mapudungun, a language isolate; they refer to descendants of Spanish colonists as wingka. Mapuche political authority was decentralized: local chiefs called lonco (alongside figures called ulmen) would meet in wartime to elect a single temporary military leader, the toqui or toki, and prominent chiefs employed silversmiths called ruxafe. They repeatedly negotiated formal parliaments with the Spanish, including the Treaty of Quillin, at which outsiders were made to bury their weapons and plant a canelo tree; the northern branch's lack of advanced agriculture led northern neighbors to nickname them "promauca" (wild wolves), and before Spanish contact they had already halted the Inca Empire's southward expansion at the Maule River after adopting agricultural techniques from Tiwanaku, though some worked the Marga-Marga mine to pay tribute to the Inca. The defining conflict of Mapuche history is the Arauco War against Spain: at the Battle of Tucapel, warriors using giant clava clubs and organized spearman formations, led by the toqui Lautaro, killed the conquistador Pedro de Valdivia, who had earlier mutilated captured warriors by cutting off their toes at El Valle de la Mocha. After Lautaro's death, Caupolican led continued resistance; individual acts of Mapuche defiance recur in trivia, from a warrior named Colo Colo (now the namesake of a popular Chilean soccer club) to a warrior who kicked an executioner off a platform to leap onto a spike himself, and another who, after having both hands amputated, tied knives to his stumps to keep fighting. The Spanish poet Alonso de Ercilla sympathetically fictionalized Lautaro and the war in his epic La Araucana, now Chile's national epic, and the "Lieutenant Nun" Catalina de Erauso earned an ensign's rank fighting the Mapuche while passing as a man. In 1598, the leader Pelantaro's uprising culminated in the Battle of Curalaba, after which the Mapuche destroyed a group of Spanish settlements south of the Bio-Bio River known as the Seven Cities, temporarily expelling the Spanish and later fighting alongside the losing Royalists in the Guerra a muerte ("War to the Death"). Mapuche warfare centered on the malon, a mounted cattle raid adopted after they acquired the horse; the historian Guillaume Boccara classified their military actions into vindictive tautulun attacks, defensive weichan wars, and rapid supply raids, and the leader Lientur favored feigned retreats. Distinctive cultural practices included the martial art Kollelaullin and, before colonial contact, plowing with the now-extinct chilihueque camelid. The Mapuche retained independence south of the Bio-Bio River throughout the entire colonial era, only losing it in the 19th century: the Frenchman Orelie-Antoine I declared himself King of Araucania, proposing a parliament modeled on their traditional coyag assembly and promising French aid; Chile ended their independence with the Occupation of Araucania, completed by Domingo Santa Maria's "final pacification" after the War of the Pacific; and across the Andes, Argentina's Conquest of the Desert under Julio Argentino Roca forcibly displaced the Mapuche and the neighboring Tehuelche (who had adopted Mapuche language and culture in a process called "Araucanization"), defended against by the trench known as Alsina's Ditch (Zanja de Alsina); earlier, mass Mapuche migrations northward had been checked by the founding of the colonial city of La Serena. In contemporary Chile and Argentina, the Mapuche remain politically active: their communal lands around Temuco were privatized under Augusto Pinochet's dictatorship, and they have protested the continued use of Pinochet-era anti-terrorism laws, while the militant group CAM, led by Hector Llaitul, seeks autonomy. The activist Elisa Loncon chaired Chile's 2021 constitutional convention, which reserved seats for the Mapuche but saw its proposed constitution rejected in a 2022 referendum; Mapuche ideas have since inspired a 2024 legal-rights declaration for the Biobio river, even as the flooding of a Mapuche graveyard by the Ralco Hydroelectric Plant and, in October 2024, Javier Milei's cancellation of a planned Argentine land cession, have kept land conflicts ongoing. The group is also served by the radio station Petu Mongelein ("we're still alive"), and activists like Betiana Colhuan continue to organize through traditional trawun assemblies.
recursive_suggestions: Pedro de Valdivia — conquistador killed by the Mapuche; recurring figure with independent clue material (heart removed per La Araucana, service under Pizarro, founding of Santiago/Concepcion/Valdivia)
Julio Argentino Roca — led the Conquest of the Desert against the Mapuche and Tehuelche; independent Argentine-history answerline
Conquest of the Desert — appeared as its own tossup answerline (2024 COOT); excluded from this page's clue count since its actual answer differs from Mapuche
Orelie-Antoine I / Kingdom of Araucania and Patagonia — recurring figure with enough independent detail (self-declared kingship, French plans) to merit its own topic
Alonso de Ercilla / La Araucana — Literature answerline; category filter to Literature to avoid overlap with this History page
Inca Empire — related but distinct topic; excluded here except where it directly overlaps with pre-conquest Mapuche history (Maule River, Marga-Marga tribute)
Tiwanaku — related but distinct topic; excluded here except where it directly overlaps via Tom Dillehay's argument about shared agricultural techniques
